questions.question: Explain the Crow's foot notation symbols used to represent (i) entity, (ii) attribute, (iii) weak entity, and (iv) identifying relationship. Also explain what the 'crowsfoot' visual indicates in a relationship line.
keyPoints: Crow's foot symbol resembles a crow's foot with three prongs representing 'many'
Circle (or optional circle) indicates optional participation (zero)
Double line indicates mandatory participation (one or more)

questions.question: Define the following with respect to ER diagrams: (i) Cardinality, (ii) Participation constraint (total vs partial), (iii) Degree of a relationship (unary, binary, ternary). Give one example for each from a university database context.
keyPoints: Cardinality specifies how many instances of an entity relate to another entity (1:1, 1:N, M:N)
Participation constraint defines whether existence of an entity depends on its association with another (total/partial)
Degree refers to number of participating entities in a relationship
Example: Student enrolls in Course (M:N), Employee manages Department (1:1)

questions.question: Consider the following ER diagram for a Library Management System: STUDENT (StudentID, Name, Address) and BOOK (BookID, Title, Author) with a relationship BORROWS (DateBorrowed, DateDue) where a student can borrow multiple books and a book can be borrowed by multiple students over time. Convert this ER diagram into relational schema(s). Clearly identify primary keys, foreign keys, and any junction table if required. Also mention the referential integrity constraints.
keyPoints: BORROWS(StudentID FK, BookID FK, DateBorrowed, DateDue, PRIMARY KEY (StudentID, BookID, DateBorrowed))
Foreign key constraints: StudentID references STUDENT(StudentID), BookID references BOOK(BookID)

questions.question: (a) Compare Chen's ER notation with Crow's foot notation, highlighting advantages and limitations of each. (b) Draw a UML class diagram representation for a ternary relationship TEACHES(Professor, Course, Semester) where a professor teaches one course per semester, a course is taught by one professor per semester, and each semester has multiple courses. Show multiplicity annotations at each end.
keyPoints: Chen's notation uses rectangles for entities, diamonds for relationships, ovals for attributes - more abstract and theoretical
Crow's foot uses boxes for entities, lines for relationships - more practical and intuitive for database design
Crow's foot clearly shows cardinality with visual symbols (crow's foot = many, single line = one)
UML multiplicity: Professor (0..*), Course (0..*), Semester (0..*) for ternary; or 1 at professor and course ends if exactly one professor teaches one course per semester
